name: 47 ¬∑ Stream Object Detection (video data channel ‚Üí YOLO26 ‚Üí detections control stream)
description:
The AI capstone of the streaming arc: the first workflow that CONSUMES a data stream and TRANSFORMS it into a control stream. A real video flows over a binary DATA channel; a YOLO26 detector pulls it off the wire, recognizes objects frame-by-frame, and emits a CONTROL stream of recognized objects which a consumer-edge `join: gather` folds into an aggregate report.

  Start{video: file} ‚Üí stream (retrieve clip, re-mux to fragmented MP4/H.264, write over Data/Out `video`) ‚Üí detector (stream('video') ‚Üí YOLO26 + ByteTrack on the Apple-Silicon GPU ‚Üí emit each object on Control/Out `detections`) ‚Üí summary (gather `detections` ‚Üí per-class counts) ‚Üí End.

The video is a real uploaded file, NOT base64. The Start `video` field is `kind: file`, so `start.video` is a FileRef; the `stream` step calls `aithericon.file(start.video['key']).retrieve()` to broker the bytes from the object store through the sidecar, decodes them with PyAV, and re-muxes a 480√ó360 H.264 clip into a FRAGMENTED MP4 byte stream (content_type `video/mp4;codecs="avc1.42E01E"`) written over the `video` data channel ‚Äî so the same bytes both feed the detector AND can be played live in a `<video>` element via Media Source Extensions (the demo-46 presentation path). The bulk video rides the out-of-band JetStream datastream transport ‚Äî it NEVER enters the net marking; the net sees only the channel's open + close.

The `detector` step drains the byte stream via `stream('video')` (starting EARLY, while the stream step is still producing), buffers it, and runs YOLO26 ‚Äî Ultralytics' latest SOTA real-time object detector (NMS-free / native end-to-end) ‚Äî with ByteTrack for stable per-object track IDs. Inference runs on the M-series GPU via the PyTorch MPS backend (CPU fallback), comfortably real-time. Rather than returning one blob, it EMITS each recognized object the moment its frame is processed onto the Control/Out channel `detections` via `aithericon.out('detections')` (open ‚Üí item* ‚Üí close): one tiny `{frame,t,label,confidence,bbox,track_id}` token per object. The producer does NOT decide the fold; the `detector ‚Üí summary` edge carries `join: gather`, so the compiler's gather barrier re-orders the detections by index and parks the collection as `{output: [...]}`. The `summary` step reads that off `input.output` and tallies totals, a per-class histogram (person/car/truck/bus/‚Ä¶), distinct tracked objects, and the peak per-frame count. AI-driven streamed processing emitting a stream of recognized objects ‚Äî the data-plane ‚Üí AI ‚Üí control-plane loop closed end-to-end.

A self-contained sample is bundled at `assets/clip.mp4` (a 4 s 480√ó360 10 fps H.264 clip of a pedestrian plaza, derived from OpenCV's `vtest.avi`). YOLO26 reliably finds people and vehicles in it (‚âà6‚Äì11 objects/frame). Run it with one command ‚Äî `mekhan run demos/47-stream-object-detection --start-tokens demos/47-stream-object-detection/sample-input.json` ‚Äî where the bundled `sample-input.json` ships `{"video": {"__file": "assets/clip.mp4"}}`; the CLI uploads the file and substitutes the FileRef.

LIVE-ONLY (run via the API, not `mekhan test`): the `detector` step first pip-installs `ultralytics` (PyTorch + OpenCV, a few hundred MB) and downloads `yolo26n.pt` (~5 MB) into the venv/weights cache, which exceeds the 60 s test cap on a cold run; warm runs are fast. SOTA note: YOLO26 (Ultralytics, NMS-free / native end-to-end, edge-optimized) is the latest in the line ‚Äî YOLO11/12 and RT-DETR are also real-time SOTA; on Apple Silicon, converting to Core ML (ANE) is the fastest path, but PyTorch-MPS keeps the demo a pure pip install and is plenty for real-time at this resolution.
